summary: Jeff Atwood examines the emerging potential of VR technology, sparked by John Carmack leaving id Software to become CTO of Oculus. He attributes the timing of consumer VR's viability to the 'peace dividend of the smartphone wars' – cheap, powerful components that were once military-grade technology. While enthusiastic about legendary programmers Carmack and Abrash working on VR, Atwood tempers expectations with his honest assessment of the current Oculus Rift developer kit's significant limitations: low resolution, cumbersome setup, lack of content, and anti-social nature. Despite these concerns, he remains cautiously optimistic that VR could see a breakthrough similar to Doom or Quake.
key_points: John Carmack leaving id Software for Oculus signals VR's serious potential – you don't abandon the company you founded without compelling reasons
Consumer VR became possible because smartphone component innovation made formerly 'unobtainium' military technology available cheaply
The current developer kit has major issues: extremely low effective resolution, complex setup, limited game support, and antisocial usage patterns
Having programming legends like Carmack and Abrash working on VR suggests we may be approaching a killer app breakthrough
True VR immersion requires many additional systems beyond the headset: positional audio, hand tracking, body tracking, potentially treadmills
money_quotes: An id Software without Carmack is like an Apple without Woz and Jobs.
Virtual reality is the stuff of programmer legend. Every software engineer that's ever read Snow Crash has dreamed of jacking into the metaverse.
They were essentially 'unobtainium' 10 years ago. This is stuff that used to be military industrial technology; you can buy it at RadioShack now.
I thought VR would be at best a novelty in my lifetime.
We could be on the verge of a Doom or Quake style killer app breakthrough in VR.
VR is a surprisingly anti-social hobby, even by gamer standards, which are, uh… low.
Nothing is quite as boring as watching another person sit down, strap on a headset, and have an extended VR 'experience.'
If you want some of the hardest practical problems in computer science to work on, bringing VR to the world is as ambitious (and fun!) a goal in software and hardware engineering I can think of.
tone: cautiously optimistic, technically grounded, enthusiastic yet skeptical
key_insight: The convergence of cheap smartphone components and legendary programming talent at Oculus creates unprecedented potential for consumer VR, though significant technical and social challenges remain before it becomes truly compelling.
